ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for sugar syrup as a food item; SNOMED CT coverage for generic processed syrups is limited.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of sugar syrup is approximately 1 tablespoon (20 g), commonly used as a sweetener added to beverages, desserts, or baked goods.

nutrient_source.title: Sugar syrup — Singapore HPB Foodo Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Values taken directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for sugar syrup (cr_id F13030010). All core macros and secondary nutrients are sourced from this record with confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: Sucrose — Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values
gi_source.source_match_notes: Sugar syrup is predominantly sucrose dissolved in water. The GI of sucrose is established at approximately 65 (medium) per the University of Sydney GI Database. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (65 × 74.9 × 0.20) / 100 = 9.74, rounded to 9.7, based on a 20 g serving containing approximately 14.98 g available carbohydrate.

hypertension.key_factor: Extreme sugar content (74.9 g/100 g) with zero dietary fibre drives insulin-mediated sodium retention and raises blood pressure risk; elevated sodium at 130 mg/100 g is an additional concern.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals managing hypertension should avoid sugar syrup entirely, as its very high sucrose concentration promotes insulin-driven sodium retention and contributes to elevated blood pressure independent of direct sodium intake; the potassium value (610 mg/100 g) may reflect a molasses-containing variant and should not be relied upon as a compensatory benefit in plain sugar syrup.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar at 74.9 g/100 g with zero dietary fibre and a medium GI of 65 produces rapid and substantial postprandial blood glucose elevation; even a 20 g serving delivers approximately 15 g of pure sucrose directly into the bloodstream.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid sugar syrup entirely, as its near-pure sucrose composition (74.9 g sugar/100 g, 0 g fibre) causes immediate and significant glycaemic spikes that are incompatible with blood glucose management targets.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Very high free sugar content (74.9 g/100 g) with no fibre, no beneficial fats, and no micronutrient density promotes dyslipidaemia, hepatic de novo lipogenesis, and elevated triglycerides, all of which are established cardiovascular risk factors per AHA guidelines.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with or at risk of cardiovascular disease should strictly limit or eliminate sugar syrup from their diet, as its concentrated sucrose content raises triglycerides, promotes visceral adiposity, and contributes to cardiometabolic risk without providing any cardioprotective nutrients.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Concentrated refined sugar (74.9 g/100 g) with zero fibre and no anti-inflammatory factors promotes systemic inflammation via upregulation of pro-inflammatory cytokines (IL-6, TNF-alpha) relevant to rheumatoid arthritis disease activity.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid sugar syrup entirely, as its extreme sucrose concentration actively worsens inflammatory load and provides no anti-inflammatory benefit whatsoever.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Sugar syrup is a processed liquid sweetener produced by dissolving refined sucrose (table sugar) in water, typically under heat, to form a clear, viscous, intensely sweet solution. It is one of the most concentrated sugar sources in the food supply, containing approximately 74.9 g of sugar per 100 g with zero dietary fibre, zero fat, and negligible protein (0.2 g/100 g). The energy density is 288 kcal per 100 g, derived almost entirely from carbohydrates. Sugar syrup is widely used as a sweetening agent in beverages, desserts, confectionery, baked goods, and cocktails. Its texture is thick and pourable, and its flavour is purely sweet with no bitterness or complexity unless derived from a molasses-containing raw sugar source. Clinically, sugar syrup is one of the most glycaemically impactful foods available: its GI of approximately 65 (medium, analogous to sucrose) combined with its extreme carbohydrate density produces a very high glycaemic load per serving. It is contraindicated for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, cardiovascular disease, and rheumatoid arthritis. The sodium content of 130 mg/100 g and the potassium value of 610 mg/100 g reported in the HPB database may reflect a molasses-containing or dark sugar variant rather than plain refined sugar syrup; plain sugar syrup typically has negligible mineral content. Iron at 3.3 mg/100 g and calcium at 230 mg/100 g similarly suggest a raw or unrefined sugar base. Nutritionally, sugar syrup provides empty calories with no fibre, no beneficial fats, no vitamins, and no meaningful protein, making it nutritionally poor despite its high energy content.
common_user_queries: How many calories in sugar syrup?
What is the GI of sugar syrup?
Is sugar syrup the same as simple syrup?
How much sugar is in sugar syrup?
Is sugar syrup bad for blood pressure?
Can I use sugar syrup if I have heart disease?
What is sugar syrup made of?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F13030010) with a reported confidence of 0.95. GI is estimated from the well-established sucrose GI value (65) from the University of Sydney GI Database, which is the closest defensible analogous food for a sucrose-dominant syrup. All values are internally consistent and plausible for a concentrated sugar solution.
notes: fat_g is confirmed zero by formulation: sugar syrup is produced solely from sucrose and water, containing no fat by composition. This is confirmed zero per HPB Singapore Foodo database. dietary_fibre_g is confirmed zero: refined sucrose contains no dietary fibre. cholesterol_mg is confirmed zero: sugar syrup is a plant-derived product with no cholesterol. saturated_fat_g is confirmed zero by formulation consistent with fat_g being zero. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid, vitamin, and moisture data for this specific sugar syrup variant are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for F13030010. The elevated potassium (610 mg/100 g), calcium (230 mg/100 g), iron (3.3 mg/100 g), and sodium (130 mg/100 g) values reported by HPB may reflect a molasses-containing or dark raw sugar variant rather than plain refined white sugar syrup; plain refined sugar syrup typically has negligible mineral content. These values are preserved as reported by HPB but flagged for review. GI of 65 is an analogous estimate based on sucrose GI from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for this specific HPB sugar syrup formulation was found in the approved GI database.
